caveats: a: No source allocates portrait-studio payroll by task or measures AI substitution directly.
a: The O*NET occupation also includes commercial, scientific, and journalistic photographers, and does not represent the narrowed lens exactly.
rho: This is an implementation judgment, not an observed industry adoption rate.
rho: Implementation may save owner time without reducing payroll when owners perform both production and administration.
e: The injected LMM firm count is not re-estimated here.
e: Census materials establish available demographic and receipt-size tabulations but do not report the lens-eligible share for this NAICS.
s5: No cited source reports control-transfer probability for portrait studios.
s5: Retirements, closures, and asset sales without continuing operations are excluded from the intended construct.
q: O*NET competition evidence is for the broader photographer occupation rather than the narrowed firm lens.
q: No source measures contractual pass-through or price elasticity for portrait studios.
d5: Employment is not demand quantity and the BLS projection is a ten-year, broad-occupation measure.
d5: The forecast does not isolate recurring portrait work or hold quality constant in the manner required by this primitive.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for portrait studios.
o: O*NET describes the wider photographer occupation, including activities outside the narrowed lens.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The kept firms sell customer-funded portrait sessions or recurring program contracts, normally as a fixed session, package, print, or digital-delivery price rather than cost-plus reimbursement.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence: the NAICS definition combines recurring school or studio relationships with one-off wedding, event, passport, still, and video work, whose repeat pattern, transferability, and billing differ materially.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off special-event and wedding-only operators, standalone passport-photo counters, hobbyist or nonemployer practices, captive photography units, and financing vehicles are excluded.
lens.included_activities: Independent portrait studios supplying repeat or recurring external services, including school and daycare picture programs, recurring corporate headshot programs, and repeat family or milestone portrait sessions; still and video work are included when attached to those relationships.

caveats: No source allocates portrait-studio payroll by task or measures AI substitution directly.
The O*NET occupation also includes commercial, scientific, and journalistic photographers, and does not represent the narrowed lens exactly.
a.change_evidence: Time-and-motion data or payroll-coded time sheets from a representative sample of portrait studios, split among shooting, editing, sales, scheduling, and administration, plus observed post-adoption staffing changes.
a.rationale: The photographer task inventory includes retouching, resizing, selecting images, transferring and archiving files, scheduling, bookkeeping, and client communication, all of which can be materially assisted by AI or workflow automation. Capturing, lighting, directing people, customer service, and aesthetic judgment remain central paid work, so the exposed wage-weighted share is well below one-half.

d5.bridge: S3 measures U.S. employment for the broad Photographer occupation, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity demanded from the narrowed portrait-studio service basket. Its 2024-to-2034 employment projection is converted only into a directional five-year demand anchor; it likely overstates durability for portrait studios because it includes commercial and other photographers, while employment can also understate demand when productivity changes.
caveats: Employment is not demand quantity and the BLS projection is a ten-year, broad-occupation measure.
The forecast does not isolate recurring portrait work or hold quality constant in the manner required by this primitive.
d5.change_evidence: Annual contract-volume, session-count, and inflation-adjusted receipts data for school, family, and corporate portrait operators; school enrollment-linked portrait order data; and local consumer survey evidence on substituting smartphones or AI-generated imagery.
d5.rationale: BLS projects broad photographer employment from 151,200 in 2024 to 154,000 in 2034, while explicitly saying portrait demand remains and smartphone quality can reduce professional demand. For the narrower current-service basket, a modest five-year decline is the central case, with a range allowing resilient school and milestone demand.

caveats: The injected LMM firm count is not re-estimated here.
Census materials establish available demographic and receipt-size tabulations but do not report the lens-eligible share for this NAICS.
e.change_evidence: A company-level NAICS 541921 list with revenue mix, customer concentration, owner involvement, employee count, and normalized EBITDA, followed by direct qualification calls.
e.rationale: The official classification includes home, school, passport, wedding, still, video, and studio portrait services. After removing the one-off, hobbyist, captive, and non-transferable portion, only a minority to a little over half of LMM firms plausibly fit the repeat-service operator lens.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $1,850.6M / sectoral output (T30) $9,021.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $121,356 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541921); class <5: 8,742 firms, avg revenue/firm $199,817 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,271 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 561 firms, avg revenue/firm $841,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $131,628 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 221 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,410,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $220,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 123 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,717,894 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $581,850 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,950,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,026,675 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,742,571 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,105,712 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 21

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for portrait studios.
O*NET describes the wider photographer occupation, including activities outside the narrowed lens.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and discrete-choice surveys across schools, families, and employers; procurement records identifying self-service or software substitution; and a sample of studio workflows distinguishing operator-required capture from automated post-production.
o.rationale: A substantial share of portrait demand still requires a human operator to organize sessions, direct subjects, set lighting, capture images, manage consent, and deliver a trusted customer experience. Smartphones and software can eliminate lower-complexity demand and much of post-production, so the share is not assumed to be near one.

caveats: O*NET competition evidence is for the broader photographer occupation rather than the narrowed firm lens.
No source measures contractual pass-through or price elasticity for portrait studios.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level quote, package-price, conversion, labor-hour, and gross-margin data before and after workflow automation, benchmarked against local competitors.
q.rationale: Fixed session and package pricing allows a studio to retain some editing and administrative efficiency until renewal or the next quote. However, the work is consumer- and customer-facing and O*NET reports that 52 percent of photographers rate competition as extremely competitive, making pass-through, competitor imitation, and lower package prices likely over five years.

caveats: This is an implementation judgment, not an observed industry adoption rate.
Implementation may save owner time without reducing payroll when owners perform both production and administration.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of portrait studios recording generative-editing, culling, CRM, and scheduling adoption; QA rejection rates; and hours or headcount removed from each workflow.
rho.rationale: Editing, selection, customer-response drafting, scheduling, and file administration can be implemented in common digital workflows within five years, but studios must protect image quality, consent, likeness, brand style, and the client experience. The small, self-employed operating model noted by BLS may also limit process redesign and measured hiring avoidance.

caveats: No cited source reports control-transfer probability for portrait studios.
Retirements, closures, and asset sales without continuing operations are excluded from the intended construct.
s5.change_evidence: Transaction data from BizBuySell, DealStats, or Axial filtered to portrait studios; owner-age distributions from detailed ABS and NES-D tables; and interviews with specialty small-business brokers about completed versus withdrawn listings.
s5.rationale: A five-year control transfer is plausible for some owner-operated studios, but personal reputation, owner-led shooting, seasonal customer relationships, and limited separation of the operator from the asset base constrain qualifying transfers. Census makes owner-age and ownership data obtainable for employer and nonemployer businesses, but the available sources do not measure five-year control transfers for this lens.

principal_driver: Automatable post-production and administrative workflows are embedded in a service that still needs human capture and client interaction.
principal_weakness: The NAICS code contains many owner-dependent and one-off businesses, while smartphones and software can erode lower-complexity portrait demand.
